statement: Replacing scan-and-dispatch scheduling with an explicit run-unit queue table plus OCC-guarded claim/lease workers will reduce duplicate processing risk and improve throughput predictability under concurrency.
rationale: Current code already approximates lease claims at batch level, while external queue systems and Convex docs indicate at-least-once/retry behavior should be handled with explicit leases, idempotency, and poison-message handling.
keyAssumptions: Run processing can be decomposed into independently claimable run units with deterministic completion criteria.
A due-indexed queue table can be queried and claimed with bounded read cost compared with current status scans.
Unit-level idempotency keys can be enforced for provider submit/poll/finalize side effects.
validationCriteria: Under concurrent scheduler/worker load, duplicate terminal updates per run unit drop versus baseline.
P95 time-to-start for queued units remains stable as total in-flight work grows.
OCC retry and conflict rates remain acceptable at target parallelism.
Poison units transition to a dead-letter state after max receives and do not block healthy throughput.
critiquePoints: Queue-table architecture can increase implementation complexity and observability burden.
If current lease logic is already sufficient and contention is low, gains may be marginal.
Improper claim batching may shift bottlenecks to dequeue/query hotspots.
questions: What are current duplicate-processing and OCC conflict baselines in production-like load?
Should lease extension/heartbeat be added, or should leases remain short with pure retry?
What should be the dead-letter policy and replay tooling for failed run units?
uncertainties: Observed frequency of workflows exceeding the 30s lease window is unknown.
Measured impact of queue-table read/write amplification on Convex function limits is unknown.
Degree of existing idempotency in downstream result-apply handlers is not fully verified.
